text:
A couple of slips are present in the normally reliable introductory scholarship of this volume. One, in accordance with a longstanding piece of historical folklore, pertains to the Bachelor of Arts title supposedly afforded Marlowe the right to style himself "Sir Christopher". This has been an enduring tradition in the Marlowe literature since Constance Kuriyama’s 2002 biography, Sir Walter Ralegh, yet it is fundamentally wrong-headed, again conflating an honorific academic title, rather like the Latin Dominus, with the ranks of a knighthood. Marlowe cannot be called “Sir Christopher” in the same way as in *Twelfth Night*, for instance, a scholar can be styled “Sir Topas” (where it is an affectation of disguise) whilst not remotely being a real knight of the same standing as the self-styled *Sir *Toby and *Sir *Andrew.

Second, the piece makes short shrift of what it disparagingly calls “conspiracy theories,” by which it appears to mean serious historical arguments, such as those advanced by David Riggs in Heroic Tragedy, which regard the fatal stabbing of playwright Christopher Marlowe as an assassination linked to his espionage work for, perhaps, both the English Spy agencies and foreign powers. Rather than accepting any of these or similar notions, the author prefers to wave the whole business away with a curt label that it has no business applying to genuine and plausible historical speculation.
explanation: The passage reads like a precise scholarly critique with specific references and arguments that suggest a human author familiar with the literature; AI could imitate this, but the nuanced evaluation and niche citations make human authorship more likely.
